intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards respond better to playful, encouraging micro-interactions (checkmarks, progress badges) rather than clinical data displays
facade.label: Cheerful checklist style
facade.acceptImplies: Lean toward warm, motivational voice; celebrate small wins; use conversational language and visual rewards
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward clinical precision; prioritize data density and neutral tone; minimize emotional design elements

facade.hypothesis: Users building personal health dashboards may prefer breathing room and visual simplicity over dense information architecture, reducing cognitive load during routine tracking.
facade.label: Minimal charts, lots of space
facade.acceptImplies: Favor open layouts, generous whitespace, understated data viz, and slower visual rhythm.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants tighter information density, more graphical richness, or busier visual energy to feel engaged.

evidence.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards respond better to playful, encouraging micro-interactions (checkmarks, progress badges) rather than clinical data displays
evidence.implication: Lean toward warm, motivational voice; celebrate small wins; use conversational language and visual rewards

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard with gentle, curved corners and subtle shadows will feel more approachable and less clinical than sharp-edged designs
facade.axisTargeted: Softness vs. Sharpness in visual treatment
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward warm, forgiving aesthetics—avoid harsh medical-software look
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer cleaner, more structured geometric forms—or finds rounded elements feel imprecise for data tracking

facade.hypothesis: Fluid animated transitions for revealing/hiding experiment details reduce cognitive friction and create a sense of continuous flow rather than discrete state changes
facade.axisTargeted: Animated transitions vs instant state changes
facade.label: Smooth slide-in panels
facade.acceptImplies: User values graceful motion and continuity; dashboard should use slide, fade, and expand animations for all panel reveals, form submissions, and data state shifts
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers instant, snappy feedback without motion; dashboard should show/hide elements instantly with no transition delays

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar for navigation pairs better with spacious card layouts for health metrics, reducing cognitive load and allowing quick glances at progress without feeling cluttered
facade.label: Soft sidebar, airy cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values scannable, breathing room in their dashboard—likely prefers progressive disclosure and visual rest over information maximalism
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer compact views that fit more data per screen, or alternative navigation patterns like tabs that don't consume vertical space

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer soft, ambient visualization of health metrics over stark data presentation, finding emotional resonance more motivating than clinical precision
facade.axisTargeted: Emotional tone of data display (clinical vs. warm)
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Explore calming color palettes, rounded components, progress visualizations that feel encouraging rather than evaluative
facade.rejectImplies: Explore crisp, high-contrast data presentation; lean into clarity and precision; consider dark/neutral aesthetic; prioritize information density over comfort

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards may feel intimidated or demotivated by traditional medical-style data presentation (rows, columns, numbers-first layouts), and will resonate more with narrative or visual-first alternatives that contextualize metrics within their personal story
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — clinical/detached vs warm/personal
facade.label: Clinical data table
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into data-heavy, precise, medical-adjacent presentation; users trust authority and clarity over encouragement; embrace rows, technical language, clinical framing
facade.rejectImplies: Avoid sterile layouts; users want their health experiments to feel personal and motivating, not like a hospital intake form; prioritize narrative context and emotional resonance over pure information density
